Bamboo can grow up to 91 cm in a single day, making it the fastest growing plant on Earth.
The Monstera's iconic leaf holes are called fenestrations, evolved to let light reach lower leaves.
Overwatering kills more houseplants than underwatering — most plants prefer to dry out slightly between waterings.
A single mature oak tree can absorb up to 100 gallons of water per day through its roots.
The Venus flytrap is native only to a small region of North and South Carolina in the United States.
Sunflowers are heliotropic when young — their stems track the sun from east to west each day.
The oldest living tree is a Great Basin bristlecone pine in California, over 5,000 years old.
Orchids produce no nectar and trick pollinators into visiting through scent and color mimicry.
A rubber tree plant (Ficus elastica) will drip a milky latex sap if a leaf is torn — the sap is toxic to pets.
The corpse flower (Amorphophallus titanum) only blooms once every several years and smells like rotting flesh.
Pothos (Epipremnum aureum) can purify indoor air by absorbing toxins like formaldehyde and benzene.
The baobab tree stores up to 120,000 litres of water in its trunk to survive droughts.
Moss has no true roots — it absorbs water directly through its leaves.
Peace lilies are one of the few houseplants that will bloom in low light conditions.
The leaves of a ZZ plant (Zamioculcas zamiifolia) are coated in a waxy substance that reduces water loss.
Cacti are not the only succulents — all cacti are succulents, but not all succulents are cacti.
The strangler fig wraps around a host tree over decades, eventually killing it by blocking its light.
Spider plants are among the easiest houseplants to propagate — their 'babies' root in water within weeks.
Titan arum (the corpse flower) produces the largest unbranched inflorescence of any plant, reaching 3 m tall.
Lavender was used by ancient Romans in their baths — the name comes from the Latin lavare, meaning 'to wash'.
The sensitive plant (Mimosa pudica) folds its leaves inward within seconds of being touched.
Aloe vera gel contains over 75 active compounds including vitamins, minerals, and amino acids.
A snake plant (Dracaena trifasciata) releases oxygen at night, making it a popular bedroom plant.
The banyan tree can cover several acres — a single tree in India spans over 2 hectares.
Tulip bulbs can be stored dry for months and still bloom — they need cold stratification to flower.
Coffee plants produce caffeine as a natural insecticide to deter pests from eating their leaves.
Ferns are among the oldest plants on Earth, with fossil records dating back 360 million years.
The passionflower was named by Spanish missionaries who saw the blooms as symbols of Christ's Passion.
Carnivorous plants like sundews and pitcher plants evolved in nutrient-poor soils where nitrogen is scarce.
Eucalyptus trees release aromatic oils that can cause spontaneous combustion in extreme heat.
Rubber plants prefer to be slightly rootbound — repotting too often can stress them.
The Amazon water lily (Victoria amazonica) can support the weight of a child on its surface.
Vanilla flavoring comes from the seedpod of an orchid — Vanilla planifolia.
Ivy (Hedera helix) can damage brick and mortar walls over time by growing into small cracks.
Pothos leaves grow larger when the plant is allowed to climb — aerial roots help it anchor to surfaces.
The rafflesia produces the world's largest individual flower, up to 1 metre wide and 10 kg in weight.
Most succulents go dormant in summer and winter — avoid fertilising during dormancy.
The weeping fig (Ficus benjamina) is notorious for dropping leaves when moved to a new location.
Desert rose (Adenium obesum) is toxic to humans and animals if any part is ingested.
Plants grown under artificial light may need turning regularly to prevent them leaning toward the source.
The oldest known potted plant is a Cycas revoluta that has been growing in Kew Gardens since 1775.
Mint spreads aggressively via underground runners — growing it in a pot prevents it taking over a garden.
Photosynthesis converts about 1% of the solar energy a leaf receives into chemical energy.
Bird of paradise (Strelitzia reginae) can live for decades and gets more spectacular as it matures.
Soil compaction prevents oxygen from reaching roots — periodically aerating houseplant soil improves health.
Agave plants spend decades growing before flowering once, then die — this is called monocarpy.
The dragon blood tree of Socotra produces a dark red resin historically used as dye and medicine.
Fiddle-leaf figs (Ficus lyrata) are sensitive to cold draughts — keep them away from air-conditioning vents.
Peat-based potting mixes retain moisture well but can become hydrophobic when they fully dry out.
The pitcher plant digests insects using enzymes similar to those in the human stomach.
String of pearls (Senecio rowleyanus) stores water in its spherical leaves to survive dry spells.
Houseplant roots need oxygen — pots with drainage holes and well-aerated soil prevent root rot.
The quaking aspen is considered the largest organism on Earth — one clone in Utah spans 43 hectares.
Some species of bamboo flower synchronously every 60–130 years, then all die after seeding.
Terracotta pots are breathable and help prevent overwatering by allowing moisture to evaporate through the walls.
The coco de mer palm produces the largest seed of any plant — a single seed can weigh 25 kg.
Maidenhair ferns need consistently moist soil and high humidity — they're among the trickier indoor ferns.
The prayer plant (Maranta leuconeura) folds its leaves upward at night, as if in prayer.
English ivy can live over 400 years in the wild and is considered invasive in many parts of North America.
Most houseplants stop actively growing in winter and need less water and no fertiliser until spring.
The saguaro cactus can live for 150 years and doesn't grow its first arm until it is 75 years old.
Chlorophyll breaks down in autumn, revealing the yellow and orange pigments that were always in the leaf.
The world's smallest flowering plant is Wolffia globosa, roughly the size of a grain of sand.
Neem tree extracts are widely used as organic pesticides — they disrupt insect growth cycles without harming birds.
Calathea plants are nicknamed 'prayer plants' because their leaves also move in response to light cycles.
Begonias are one of the largest genera of flowering plants, with over 1,800 species.
Plants in terracotta pots need watering more frequently than those in plastic or ceramic pots.
The papyrus plant was used by ancient Egyptians to make paper — the word 'paper' derives from it.
Rosemary and lavender are drought-tolerant herbs — they prefer sandy, well-draining soil.
Wisteria can live for over 100 years and a single plant can cover an entire building.
The pitcher plant genus Nepenthes has been found to digest rats, lizards, and even small birds.
Yellow leaves on a houseplant most often signal overwatering, not a nutrient deficiency.
The kapok tree can grow up to 60 m tall and its fibrous seed pods were historically used to stuff life jackets.
Succulents need bright, indirect light for at least 6 hours a day to avoid etiolation (stretching toward light).
Staghorn ferns are epiphytes — in nature they grow on other plants, not in soil.
The scent of petrichor (rain on dry earth) is produced partly by plant oils absorbed into the soil.
Dracaena marginata is one of the most air-purifying plants according to NASA's Clean Air Study.
Root rot, caused by the water mould Phytophthora, is the most common killer of overwatered houseplants.
The cork oak tree can be harvested for its bark every 9 years without harming the tree.
Roses have been cultivated for over 5,000 years — they are among the most ancient cultivated flowers.
The jade plant (Crassula ovata) is said in feng shui to bring prosperity and is easy to care for in bright light.
Trailing houseplants like pothos and heartleaf philodendron grow longer and lusher when allowed to hang freely.
The Christmas cactus (Schlumbergera) is triggered to bloom by long nights and cooler temperatures in autumn.
Oak trees produce tannins that make their fallen leaves slow to decompose, creating durable leaf litter.
The titan arum's heat generation during bloom (up to 36°C) helps disperse its scent over a wide area.
Spider plant (Chlorophytum comosum) is non-toxic to cats and dogs, making it a safe choice for pet owners.
Using a moisture meter is more reliable than a watering schedule — plants need water when the soil is dry, not by the calendar.
Monstera deliciosa produces an edible fruit that tastes like a blend of banana, pineapple, and coconut.
The welwitschia plant of the Namib Desert is the sole surviving member of its order and can live over 1,000 years.
Cacti in cold climates survive frost by allowing their cells to dehydrate — ice forms between cells, not inside them.
The lotus flower closes at night and sinks below the water, reopening with the morning sun.
Perlite added to potting soil improves drainage and aeration, particularly for succulents and cacti.
Many tropical houseplants, including monsteras and philodendrons, are toxic to cats and dogs if ingested.
The black bat flower (Tacca chantrieri) produces extraordinary dark blooms that resemble bats in flight.
Overfeeding a houseplant causes 'fertiliser burn' — salt accumulation that draws water out of the roots.
The tallest living tree is Hyperion, a coastal redwood in California measuring 115.9 m tall.
Boston ferns require high humidity and are a classic choice for bathrooms with natural light.
The Antarctic pearlwort (Colobanthus quitensis) is one of only two flowering plants that grow on Antarctica.
Watering in the morning reduces fungal disease risk — foliage has time to dry before cooler night air arrives.
The sacred fig (Ficus religiosa) is considered the tree under which the Buddha achieved enlightenment.
Oleander is one of the most toxic garden plants — all parts are poisonous, even the smoke from burning it.
Epiphyllum (orchid cactus) blooms only at night, with flowers that last just a single night.
Repotting a plant into a pot too much larger than its current one can lead to overwatering problems.
The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and contains 10% of all species on Earth.
Pineapple plants are bromeliads — the spiky fruit grows from a rosette of strap-like leaves.
Dracaena trifasciata (snake plant) is one of the few plants that continues converting CO₂ to oxygen at night.
Plants communicate through chemical signals — when damaged, they release volatile compounds to warn neighbours.
The cashew nut is the seed of the cashew apple, a tropical fruit that is itself edible but highly perishable.
The water hyacinth can double its population in two weeks, making it one of the world's worst aquatic weeds.
Pothos, spider plants, and peace lilies are reliable choices for beginners because they clearly signal thirst by drooping.
Mosses can survive complete desiccation and revive when rehydrated — some species after decades.
The black pepper plant is a vine — the spice comes from the dried berries of Piper nigrum.
Most houseplants evolved on forest floors and prefer bright, indirect light rather than direct sun.
The sea squill (Drimia maritima) produces a bulb so large it sits above ground and blooms without soil.
Grafting is commonly used with cacti — a colourful astrophytum or moon cactus is grafted onto a green stock cactus.
The ginkgo tree has been essentially unchanged for 200 million years, making it a living fossil.
Fiddle-leaf figs produce new leaves in a flush — sometimes several at once — then pause for weeks.
Nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium (N-P-K) are the three primary macronutrients plants need from fertiliser.
Plants native to dry climates store water in thick, fleshy leaves, stems, or roots.
The sandbox tree (Hura crepitans) has explosive seed pods that launch seeds at over 70 m/s.
Anthuriums produce their colourful 'flowers' for months — the true flowers are tiny bumps on the central spadix.
A healthy root system is white or tan — brown, mushy roots are a sign of rot and must be trimmed away.
The corpse lily (Rafflesia arnoldii) is a parasite with no leaves, stem, or roots of its own.
When repotting, choose a pot only 2–5 cm wider in diameter than the current one to avoid overwatering risk.
Citrus trees grown indoors need very bright light — south-facing windows or a grow light for best results.
Aspirin dissolved in water (aspirin solution) has been shown to trigger disease resistance in some plants.
The traveller's palm is not a true palm but related to birds of paradise — its leaf sheaths collect rainwater.
Terracotta pots become more porous with age, allowing moisture to escape even faster over time.
Carnivorous sundews catch insects with sticky mucilage on their tentacle-tipped leaves.
Root pruning is sometimes used to keep bonsai trees small — the technique can be applied to any potted plant.
The strangler fig begins life as an epiphyte, dropping aerial roots that eventually encase the host tree entirely.
Aloe vera requires very little water — in winter, once a month is often sufficient.
Cactus spines are modified leaves — they reduce water loss, provide shade, and deter herbivores.
The dragon fruit (pitaya) grows on a climbing cactus — the plant blooms only at night.
Humid air from pebble trays or grouping plants together can benefit moisture-loving tropical species.
Ivy and ferns are popular hanging plants because they trail gracefully and enjoy the higher humidity found near ceilings.
Aroid plants (pothos, philodendron, monstera) are among the fastest-growing houseplants in good conditions.
A plant that has filled its pot with roots is said to be 'rootbound' — time to repot into a slightly larger container.
The African violet (Saintpaulia) is one of the most popular flowering houseplants worldwide, bred in thousands of cultivars.
Misting leaves can raise localised humidity but does little to replicate the sustained humidity of a greenhouse.
The date palm has been cultivated in the Middle East for at least 5,000 years.
Some orchids form symbiotic relationships with specific fungi to germinate — without the fungus, the seed cannot sprout.
Cacti are dormant in winter and need almost no water — overwatering in winter is a leading cause of rot.
The water-storing tissue in succulent leaves is called parenchyma, a type of thin-walled plant tissue.
Rainforest plants often have drip tips — elongated leaf tips that channel water off the surface to prevent fungal growth.
The fig 'fruit' is actually a hollow receptacle containing hundreds of tiny flowers on the inside.
Tillandsia air plants absorb water and nutrients through their leaves, not their roots.
The sago palm (Cycas revoluta) is not a true palm but a cycad — one of the most ancient plant groups on Earth.
Pothos thrive in low light but grow faster and with larger leaves when given more bright, indirect light.
Weeping willows have shallow, wide-spreading root systems that seek out water — keep them away from pipes.
The giant sequoia is the most massive tree by volume — a single specimen in California contains 1,487 cubic metres of wood.
Plant leaves can sunburn — moving an indoor plant to direct sun too quickly causes bleached or brown patches.
Chrysanthemums are grown in Japan as a symbol of longevity and are featured on the Imperial seal.
The bird's nest fern (Asplenium nidus) grows in the junctions of tropical trees and collects debris for nutrients.
Watering with lukewarm water is gentler for tropical houseplants — cold water can shock warm-climate roots.
The lotus plant (Nelumbo nucifera) produces seeds that remain viable for over 1,000 years.
Plants breathe through tiny pores called stomata — dusty leaves block them, so occasional wiping improves health.
Rosette-forming succulents like Echeveria should not be watered into the rosette — moisture causes rot at the crown.
The petiole is the stalk that attaches a leaf blade to the stem — different from a stem in both structure and function.
Tropical houseplants generally prefer temperatures above 15°C — brief cold drafts can cause leaf drop.
The sensitive plant's rapid leaf-closing is driven by turgor pressure changes, not muscular movement.
The kiwi fruit grows on a woody vine (Actinidia deliciosa) that can live for over 50 years.
Soil pH affects nutrient availability — most houseplants prefer a slightly acidic pH of 6.0–6.5.
The traveller's palm earned its name because the water trapped in its leaf bases could quench a thirsty traveller.
Some plants produce allelopathic chemicals that inhibit the growth of competitor plants nearby.
